title: Cat Ear Discharge: What to Do if You See Pus
meta_desc: Is your cat's ear draining pus? Discover common causes, the urgency level of this symptom, and how a photo can help your vet provide better care.
content_html:
What is Pus Draining from the Ear Canal?

When you see thick, opaque discharge—often yellow, white, or green—draining from your cat's ear, it is known as purulent exudate or pus. This is a clear sign that the body is fighting a significant bacterial or yeast infection, often referred to as otitis externa or otitis media. In some cases, this drainage occurs because an underlying infection has caused the eardrum to rupture, or because of a deep abscess or inflammatory polyp within the canal.

Is It an Emergency?

The urgency level for pus draining from a cat's ear is Medium. While this is rarely a life-threatening crisis in the immediate hour, it is a painful condition that requires veterinary intervention within 24 to 48 hours. You should seek more immediate care if your cat is also exhibiting neurological signs such as a severe head tilt, stumbling, or involuntary eye movements, as these suggest the infection has reached the inner ear and is affecting their balance.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear, well-lit photo of the ear and the discharge can be an invaluable tool for your veterinary team. A photo allows a professional to assess the color, texture, and volume of the pus without having to disturb the painful ear immediately. This visual evidence helps determine the severity of the inflammation and allows the clinic to prioritize your cat's appointment based on the visible symptoms.

faqs.question: Can I clean my cat's ear with a Q-tip if I see pus?
faqs.answer: No, you should avoid using Q-tips or inserting anything into the ear canal. If the eardrum is ruptured, you could push debris deeper or cause permanent damage to your cat's hearing.

faqs.question: What are common signs that the ear infection is worsening?
faqs.answer: Watch for increased scratching, crying when the head is touched, a foul odor, or your cat acting lethargic and hiding. These indicate the pain and infection are spreading.

faqs.question: Will my cat need antibiotics for ear pus?
faqs.answer: In almost all cases, discharge consisting of pus requires prescription antibiotics, often both topical drops and oral medication, to fully clear the infection.
